planting.note: Plant scarified seeds in spring (soak in boiling water and cool overnight before planting) in well-drained, dry to average, slightly alkaline to neutral, deep soil in full sun; velvet mesquite grows naturally in desert grasslands, dry washes, and desert scrub of southern Arizona and Sonora, Mexico.

directions: Plant scarified seeds in spring in well-drained, dry to average, slightly alkaline to neutral, deep, well-aerated soil in full sun; velvet mesquite is the dominant mesquite of Arizona desert grasslands and saguaro cactus country, nearly identical to honey mesquite (P. glandulosa) but more densely hairy (velutinous) on the leaves and pods, tolerating the extreme heat and seasonal drought of the Sonoran Desert. The long, flat, tan seed pods mature in late summer and are very high in sugar and protein — edible by humans (ground into mesquite flour), consumed by coyotes, javelinas, deer, and dozens of bird species. The fragrant, cream-colored flower spikes in spring attract swarms of native bees. Deep taproots (up to 60 feet) access groundwater far below the surface. An important nitrogen-fixing tree that improves soil fertility. The aromatic wood produces some of the finest barbecue smoke in the world — prized for smoked meats throughout the Southwest.

planting.note: Plant scarified seeds in spring in well-drained, dry to moderately moist, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; screwbean mesquite grows naturally in desert washes, riverbanks, and seasonally moist floodplains of the southwestern US and Mexico. The uniquely spiraling seed pods are highly distinctive.

directions: Plant scarified seeds in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun; screwbean mesquite is immediately recognizable by its uniquely coiled, spiral seed pods — tightly twisted into a tight helix resembling a screw or corkscrew — unlike the straight or gently curved pods of other mesquites. The common name 'tornillo' (Spanish for screw) reflects this distinctive pod shape. The pods are edible, sweet, and nutritious, and were processed into food by Sonoran Desert indigenous peoples. The tree grows naturally along desert washes and dry riverbanks of the Southwest, tolerating the highly alkaline, sometimes saline soils of desert floodplains. The cream-colored flower spikes are fragrant and heavily visited by native bees. Increasingly rare in some areas due to agricultural development of desert floodplains. A distinctive, drought-tolerant native for desert Southwest gardens with excellent wildlife value.

planting.note: Plant scarified seeds in spring in well-drained, dry, rocky, slightly alkaline to neutral, calcareous soil in full sun; Anacacho orchid tree grows naturally on rocky limestone slopes and canyon walls of the Anacacho Mountains of southwest Texas and adjacent Mexico, adapted to thin, calcareous, dry soils.

directions: Plant in well-drained, rocky, slightly alkaline to neutral, calcareous soil in full sun; Anacacho orchid tree is a rare Texas native of rocky limestone canyon walls in the Anacacho Mountains of Kinney County, Texas — one of the most beautiful small native shrubs or small trees of the southwestern US. The flower display in spring is extraordinary for such a xeric plant — profuse, pure white, five-petaled, orchid-like flowers (the characteristic two-lobed leaf shape of all Bauhinia gives the entire genus the appearance of orchid plants) cover the shrub from tip to base. Unlike the tropical orchid trees widely planted in south Florida (B. purpurea, B. variegata), this species is hardy to about 15°F and adapted to dry, rocky, calcareous soils — making it far more practical for the Hill Country and Edwards Plateau. The characteristic bilobed leaf (like two fused circles) is distinctive and attractive. A rare and outstanding native ornamental.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in dry, well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; scarlet gilia is a biennial that forms a rosette in its first year and blooms in its second summer. Self-sows freely to form self-perpetuating colonies in rocky, open, sunny mountain habitats.

directions: Direct sow in fall in dry, well-drained, lean, sandy or rocky, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; scarlet gilia is one of the most spectacular wildflowers of the Rocky Mountain West, producing tall, slender stems bearing dense clusters of brilliantly scarlet, tubular flowers in midsummer — one of the primary hummingbird wildflowers of mountain meadows and open pine forests from British Columbia to Mexico. The narrow, star-shaped flowers are perfect for ruby-throated, broad-tailed, and calliope hummingbirds. The distinctive finely cut, ferny leaves and the brilliant red color make it immediately recognizable. In areas with both hummingbirds and hawkmoths, the same population shows remarkable flower color variation — red for hummingbirds, white-to-pink for hawkmoths — a famous example of selection pressure from different pollinators. Self-sows readily in open, dry, rocky conditions to form naturalized colonies in mountain or xeric gardens.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in well-drained, sandy to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; hairy phlox grows naturally in open pine woods, dry ridge-tops, and sandy to rocky upland clearings of the southeastern US. It is one of the more drought-tolerant native phlox species.

directions: Plant in well-drained, sandy to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; hairy phlox is a low-growing, spreading native phlox of sandy, dry pinelands and upland clearings of the southeastern Coastal Plain and adjacent Piedmont, producing cheerful clusters of pink to magenta-pink flowers in early spring. The distinctive sticky-hairy (hence 'hairy phlox') stems and leaves feel slightly fuzzy to the touch. More drought-tolerant and heat-tolerant than most native phlox, naturally adapted to the dry, sandy, open pinelands of the Southeast where it blooms profusely in March–April. Excellent for rock gardens, dry sandy banks, and the front of sunny to partially shaded borders in zones 5–9. Self-sows modestly in appropriate dry, sandy conditions. Combines beautifully with wild blue phlox (P. divaricata) for a spring phlox combination in transitional sun-to-shade garden settings.
